skill.name: dbcraft
skill.description: Open DB Craft, a local Windows visual schema studio for designing database models, validating structure, saving workspace models, and exporting SQL handoff artifacts.
skill.summary: Design database schemas visually, then save models and export SQL for handoff.
skill.author: laozhu001

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, environment, and weak-crypto patterns, but manual review found no prompt injection or malicious exfiltration. Most hits are markdown formatting, local localhost checks, or documented DB Craft behavior. The remaining risk is medium because the skill launches a local Windows Node app, writes workspace artifacts, and can send prompts to user-configured AI providers.

medium_findings.title: Local External Process Launch
medium_findings.description: The PowerShell launcher starts node server.js from a fixed local DBdesigner directory. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it executes code outside the skill bundle and depends on the local directory contents.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The launcher clearly invokes a local Node process. Confidence is high that this is intended behavior, but it remains a real execution risk for marketplace users.

medium_findings.title: Workspace Data Written To Local Files
medium_findings.description: The skill directs DB Craft to save models, exported SQL, and handoff notes in the active workspace. This is expected behavior, but those artifacts can include project paths, user requests, and schema details.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple docs explicitly describe local model, SQL, and handoff files. The risk is data exposure through normal artifacts, not hidden malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Optional AI Provider Data Sharing
medium_findings.description: AI table generation may send prompts and schema requests to the configured provider and may use API keys from the UI or environment. This is disclosed, but users need a clear privacy warning.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly document AI requests and API key handling. No evidence shows secret exfiltration, but third-party transmission is a real user-facing risk.

low_findings.title: Static Markdown Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: Many weak-crypto and Ruby backtick detections are caused by markdown text, menu labels, file extensions, or product copy. I found no executable Ruby, cryptographic implementation, or prompt injection in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are documentation or YAML prompt text. There is no matching executable crypto or Ruby command context at these locations.

low_findings.title: Localhost Network Checks
low_findings.description: Hardcoded network findings point to 127.0.0.1 and the local DB Craft port. This is a local readiness check, not evidence of remote exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed network behavior is bound to localhost. I did not find evidence of hardcoded external domains or remote collection endpoints in the provided files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Start-Process Runs Local Server
dangerous_patterns.description: The launcher uses Start-Process to run server.js with Node from the local application directory. This should be disclosed because it executes local code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command execution pattern is explicit and central to the launcher. It is legitimate but security-relevant.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Open With Workspace Query
dangerous_patterns.description: The launcher appends the current workspace path and name to the local launch URL after URL encoding. This can expose local path context to the local app and browser history.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code constructs a launch URL with workspacePath and workspaceName. It is local-only but still handles sensitive project context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment API Key Fallback
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill can use OPENAI_API_KEY as a fallback for AI table generation. This is common, but the marketplace listing should warn users about credential handling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment key fallback is explicitly documented. I found disclosure, but credential access is always security-relevant.

content.value_statement: Database schema work often starts as scattered notes, screenshots, and partial SQL. DB Craft turns those inputs into visual models, validation steps, and exportable SQL artifacts.
actual_capabilities: Opens a local DB Craft visual schema studio on Windows.
Creates and edits models for MySQL 8, PostgreSQL 14, SQLite, and MSSQL.
Imports CREATE TABLE scripts into the active visual model.
Generates table designs with AI when the user configures a provider key.
Saves DB model files and exports SQL in the active workspace.
Prepares local handoff files for Codex or separate migration workflows.
limitations: The current launcher is Windows and PowerShell focused.
The local DB Craft application must exist at the configured app path.
AI generation requires a user-configured provider key or a Codex handoff fallback.
The skill stops at model, SQL, and handoff artifacts; it does not deploy to live databases.

use_cases.title: Design a New Service Schema
use_cases.description: Create a visual model for service tables, relationships, indexes, and exportable SQL before implementation.

use_cases.title: Translate Requirements Into Tables
use_cases.description: Turn plain-language requirements or screenshots into a structured database model that engineers can review.

use_cases.title: Prepare Migration Handoff Artifacts
use_cases.description: Validate a schema design, save the model, and hand off SQL for a separate migration workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Open The Designer
prompt_templates.prompt: Open DB Craft for this project and tell me whether you reused an existing local service or started a new one.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Basic Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use DB Craft to create a PostgreSQL 14 model for a booking system with customers, bookings, rooms, and payments.

prompt_templates.title: Import Existing SQL
prompt_templates.prompt: Open DB Craft and import my CREATE TABLE statements into the current workspace model, then validate the structure.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare A Review Handoff
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the current DB Craft model with these schema changes, export SQL, and prepare the local handoff artifact for migration review.

output_examples.input: Create a MySQL model for customers, orders, and payments.
output_examples.output: DB Craft opens the local designer, creates the requested tables, links the order flow, saves the model, and prepares SQL for review.

output_examples.input: Import my existing table definitions and show the relationships.
output_examples.output: The skill imports the table structure into the visual workspace, validates the model, and points out relationship or naming issues.

output_examples.input: Finish this schema for a migration handoff.
output_examples.output: The skill updates the active model, exports SQL, and creates local artifacts that a migration workflow can inspect next.

best_practices: Review generated schemas before using exported SQL in any database environment.
Keep model files and exported SQL in the same project workspace.
Use provider API keys only when AI generation is needed and rotate exposed keys.
anti_patterns: Do not treat generated table ideas as production migrations without review.
Do not place secrets inside schema comments or AI prompts.
Do not assume a browser automation session saved changes until the model export completes.

faq.question: Does DB Craft run in the cloud?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow opens a local DB Craft application and a local browser page.

faq.question: Which database targets are documented?
faq.answer: The skill documents MySQL 8, PostgreSQL 14, SQLite, and MSSQL modeling workflows.

faq.question: Can it execute changes against a live database?
faq.answer: No. The public workflow stops at visual models, SQL export, and handoff artifacts.

faq.question: Does AI table generation send data to a provider?
faq.answer: Yes. Prompts and schema requests may be sent to the provider configured by the user.

faq.question: Where are model files stored?
faq.answer: Model files, SQL exports, and Codex handoff files are written in the active local workspace.

faq.question: What setup is required?
faq.answer: The current package expects Windows, PowerShell, Node.js, a browser, and the local DB Craft app files.
